context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out this reflection on progress, modernization, development, and planning, and I’m honestly a little lost on how to separate those terms. Could you help me sort out the differences first so I can build the paper around that?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — those distinctions are really at the heart of the assignment. Before we dig in, tell me: in your own words, what does 'progress' mean to you? Don't worry about being precise yet, just go with your gut.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the differences between the terms for them, which would mean doing the intellectual work of the assignment. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about what they already know or intuit about these terms, rather than giving them a taxonomy. I'll start with a simple question to activate prior knowledge.

exchanges.student: My first instinct is that progress means things getting “better” over time, but I’m not sure better for who or by whose standards. I think that’s where I get stuck, because it sounds universal at first, but maybe it’s actually political or cultural.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you just identified — universal vs. culturally specific — is exactly what the prompt is asking you to wrestle with. So let me push you a step further: do you think that tension is a modern problem, or could people in, say, ancient civilizations have had their own ideas about society 'getting better'? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has already arrived at a genuinely insightful tension — the apparent universality of progress vs. its potentially cultural/political nature. This is actually central to the assignment prompt. I should affirm the quality of this thinking briefly, then push them to connect it to the historical question in the prompt: is progress a Western/Enlightenment idea or can it be traced further back? I want them to do the intellectual work, not me.

exchanges.student: I think people in earlier societies probably did have ideas about improvement, even if they didn’t call it “progress” in the Enlightenment sense. Maybe ancient empires or religious traditions had visions of moral order, prosperity, or a well-run society, which feels related. I’m just not sure if that counts as the same concept or only as a rough precursor.
exchanges.tutor: That's a thoughtful distinction. So what do you think changed with the Enlightenment specifically? What might make the modern Western notion of 'progress' different from those earlier visions of moral order or prosperity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress on their own, identifying pre-Enlightenment ideas about societal improvement. The 'does it count as the same concept' hesitation is productive and shouldn't be resolved for them — it's actually part of the analytical work the paper requires. I should push them to think about what distinguishes those earlier ideas from the Enlightenment version, rather than resolving the question for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe the Enlightenment turns progress into something more linear and universal, like history is moving forward in a single direction through reason, science, and human control. Earlier ideas might have been more about preserving order or fulfilling a religious ideal, not this idea that all societies should advance along the same path. I think that’s the difference, but I’m not totally confident.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong distinction — linear, universal, reason-driven progress vs. earlier frameworks centered on order or divine fulfillment. Hold onto that, it'll be useful in your paper. Now, how do you think 'modernization' relates to what you just described? Is it the same idea, or does it add something different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a really strong conceptual distinction — cyclical/order-preserving vs. linear/universal progress — largely on their own. This is excellent thinking and directly relevant to the assignment. I should briefly affirm this and then pivot to connecting 'progress' to the other terms in the prompt (modernization, development, planning), since the assignment requires distinguishing all four. I don't want to dwell too long on just 'progress' when there are other key concepts to explore.

exchanges.student: I want to say modernization is like the practical version of progress, but that might be too simplistic. Maybe progress is the broader idea that history moves forward, and modernization is the process where societies are expected to become more industrial, bureaucratic, secular, or “modern” in a Western-style sense.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful distinction — progress as the broad idea, modernization as the specific pathway. Now what about 'development'? Does it feel like the same thing as modernization to you, or do you sense a difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a good conceptual chain and is moving in the right direction. Their distinction between progress as an abstract idea and modernization as a concrete process/trajectory is solid. I should affirm this briefly and then push them toward 'development' — since that's the next term in the chain and carries important political/institutional connotations that differ from modernization. I want them to discover the distinction themselves.

exchanges.student: I think development overlaps with modernization, but it feels more policy-oriented and tied to the postcolonial/postwar period. Like, modernization sounds more like a theory of social change, while development sounds like planned interventions to improve economies and living conditions. I’m not fully sure where planning fits yet though.
exchanges.tutor: You're building a solid conceptual chain. So given what you just said about development involving planned interventions — where does 'planning' fit in? Is it the same as development, or is it something more specific within it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a strong conceptual chain with real independence. Their distinction between modernization as social theory and development as planned intervention is quite good. Now they've naturally arrived at 'planning' as the remaining term. I should let them work that out rather than explaining it, by asking a targeted question. We're also approaching the point where they've explored enough conceptual ground to start integrating this into written form — I should keep this exchange productive but start nudging toward synthesis soon.

exchanges.student: I think planning is more like the tool or mechanism, not the same thing as development itself. Development is the goal or broader project, and planning is the deliberate process states or institutions use to try to get there. But I’m worried that sounds too neat, like in reality planning also carries its own ideology.
exchanges.tutor: That self-correction is actually your strongest insight so far — planning isn't ideologically neutral, and that tension is worth developing in your paper. You've now worked through all four terms. Before you start writing, what's your instinct on the last part of the prompt: the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in development? Are they opposites, or is it more complicated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced and accurate understanding — planning as a tool/mechanism with its own embedded ideology. This is excellent thinking and directly relevant to the course themes about power, legitimacy, and the state. The self-correction about ideology is particularly sharp. We've now covered all four terms reasonably well across about 6 exchanges. I should affirm this briefly and then push toward the assignment's other major questions — particularly the relationship between tradition and modernity, and whether different development traditions draw on different aspects of modernity — to make sure the paper will be complete. I should also nudge toward synthesis/writing soon.

exchanges.student: I don’t think they’re simple opposites, even though development discourse often treats them that way. My instinct is that “tradition” gets constructed as backward so “modernity” can look necessary, but in practice development planning often mixes the two or reshapes tradition rather than replacing it. Could you help me turn all this into a basic argument I can use?
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely sharp observation about tradition being constructed as 'backward' — it'll serve you well. But putting the argument together is your job, not mine. You actually have all the pieces already from our conversation. Why not try drafting a thesis statement or opening argument in your own words, and we can work from there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to construct their argument for them, which would violate the core rules about not doing the student's intellectual/writing work. However, they've done genuinely good thinking across this conversation and have the raw materials for a strong paper. I should redirect them to do the synthesis themselves, and since we've covered substantial ground across 7+ exchanges, I should push them to start writing rather than continuing to talk about it. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate what we've discussed.

exchanges.student: Okay, let me try: progress isn’t only a Western idea, because many earlier and non-Western societies had ideas about improvement, order, or collective flourishing, but the Enlightenment made progress into a more universal, linear theory of history. Then modernization, development, and planning build on that, but in different ways, and they often treat tradition as something to overcome even though the relationship is messier than that. Is that too broad?
exchanges.tutor: It's a good start and captures the arc of the argument well. The part that feels most underdeveloped is 'modernization, development, and planning build on that in different ways' — that's where your actual analysis lives, and right now it's doing a lot of work in very few words. What specific differences between those three could you name more precisely there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid working thesis that hits most of the key prompt questions. It's a good draft but it is somewhat broad and could be more specific about the distinctions between modernization, development, and planning, which they actually worked out well in conversation. I should give honest, constructive feedback without writing anything for them, and push them to sharpen the middle part specifically. I should not grade it or call it complete.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s the part I’m fuzzy on. Maybe modernization is the theory of how societies change, development is the normative project of improvement, and planning is the institutional practice used to carry it out? I’m not sure if “normative project” is the right phrase though.
exchanges.tutor: That three-part distinction is clear and defensible — trust it. As for 'normative project,' only you can decide if that phrase captures what you mean. Try using it in a sentence and see if it holds up. Now, you have a thesis and your key distinctions — it's time to start drafting. What's your first move?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a clean and defensible three-part distinction largely on their own. The 'normative project' hesitation is minor and self-correcting — I should not resolve the language question for them since the writing must be entirely theirs. I should affirm the conceptual clarity and push them toward actually integrating this into their draft rather than continuing to refine verbally. We've had 9 exchanges and are near the planned conversation length, so synthesis/writing is the priority now.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently drawing out student reasoning rather than supplying answers. Key ideas — the linear/universal nature of Enlightenment progress, the distinction between modernization/development/planning, and the constructed opposition of tradition and modernity — were all generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor explicitly declined to build the argument for the student in Turn 8, redirecting to a thesis-drafting exercise. Responses were concise, well-paced, and supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense. No significant deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never supplied final answers, conclusions, or submission-ready content; all key conceptual distinctions were elicited from the student through targeted questions. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on each student response before advancing, and used diagnostic questioning (e.g., 'what do you think changed with the Enlightenment?') rather than jumping to explanations. 1.3: The tutor provided specific process-level feedback, notably praising the student's self-correction about planning's ideology as the 'strongest insight so far' and pointing out precisely which part of the thesis was underdeveloped. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or off-topic drift, and the assignment prompt was kept central throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately brief and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
